name: eal
aliases:
  - english-as-additional-language

accessibilityFocus: >
  Designed for users whose first language is not English — removes idioms,
  culture-specific references, and complex grammatical structures that are
  difficult to parse for non-native speakers.
---

# EAL Profile (English as an Additional Language)

> Removes idiomatic, culture-specific, and grammatically complex language to
> make responses accessible to non-native English speakers.

## Communication Rules

- No idioms — replace with literal equivalents:
  "keep an eye on" → "monitor"
  "hit the ground running" → "start immediately"
  "touch base" → "check in" or "contact"
- No phrasal verbs where a single verb exists:
  "find out" → "discover" or "learn"
  "set up" → "configure" or "install"
  "put off" → "delay" or "postpone"
  "look into" → "investigate"
- No culture-specific references without a brief, neutral explanation
  (sports metaphors, regional sayings, local institutions)
- Prefer subject-verb-object sentence structure — avoid fronted clauses and
  inverted constructions where possible
- Avoid contractions in formal or instructional content ("do not" not "don't",
  "it is" not "it's") — contractions can be ambiguous for non-native readers
- Abbreviations and acronyms expanded on first use in every response
- Technical terms defined on first use — do not assume shared domain vocabulary
- Consistent terminology — never switch between synonyms for the same concept
  within a response (pick one and use it throughout)
- Plain vocabulary preferred — use the simpler word when two words mean the same thing

## Never Do

- Never use slang, colloquialisms, or informal register without signalling it
- Never assume familiarity with cultural touchstones (films, sports, regional events)
  that may not be globally known
- Never use double negatives ("not uncommon", "not unusual") — rephrase positively

## Notes

Standalone — does not extend any base profile. Compatible with all other profiles.

Shares some rules with `direct` (no idioms, explicit meaning) but serves a
different purpose: `direct` removes ambiguity for literal processors; `eal`
removes cultural and grammatical complexity for non-native speakers. They
complement each other and can be stacked.
